title: Anger at the Media That Misrepresented You
dek: When the screen reflects a distorted version of who you are, the rage doesn't just live in your mind—it settles deep into your fascia. Here is how your body processes the violence of being unseen, and how to physically reclaim your narrative.
bodyMarkdown:
It’s late afternoon, and you’re scrolling through your phone, the light in the room shifting to that cool, sterile blue hour. Then you see it: your face—or maybe it’s your community’s face—stretched across a screen or printed in ink, twisted into a narrative you don’t recognize, a story that flattens you like a shadow under glass. Your masseter tightens, that stubborn muscle at the angle of your jaw clenches involuntarily, as if trying to hold back a scream that’s lodged itself somewhere beneath your teeth. The heat rises—hot, almost electric—flush spreading from the roots of your neck up through your face, flushing your cheeks, your ears, your scalp. It’s the kind of burn that doesn’t cool down with a glass of water or a deep breath.

But the breath itself is caught, shallow and clipped, the diaphragm barely moving, as if your body is bracing for impact. You feel that familiar constriction around the solar plexus, a squeezing knot that’s been waiting for this moment—the moment when your lived truth is reduced, distorted, turned into a caricature or a villainous headline. The trapezius tightens too, knotted between your shoulders, a secret messenger of your body’s alarm. You want to look away, but something holds you fast, the compulsion to witness your own erasure, your own misrepresentation.

This isn’t just frustration or indignation. It’s a somatic storm—your body knows before your mind can fully grasp. The jaw remembers the countless times you’ve been silenced, the psoas tenses in anticipation of fight or flight. You’re not just angry; you’re registering a deep, cellular betrayal. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body speaks the truth of your experience even when the media’s story lies.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma lives in the body, that racialized and marginalized communities carry these wounds in their fascia and muscle memory. Pema Chödrön tells us that leaning into discomfort is the path to presence. And Karla McLaren shows that anger, especially the anger ignited by injustice, is a fierce and necessary messenger. So here, in this space, you don’t turn away. You lean in. You feel the heat, the tension, the breathlessness—not to be swallowed, but to be understood. Because your anger is not ugly. It’s a vital signal from the body that your story matters, that your truth demands to be seen, not distorted.

## The Somatic Reality of Misrepresentation

Your body never forgets. When you are misrepresented—whether through a dismissive glance, a silenced voice, or the twist of someone else’s story—the muscles that carry your truth begin to tighten. This is not just metaphor. The psoas, that deep core muscle anchoring your spine to your legs, clenches in a reflexive attempt to brace against the invisible weight of being unseen. It’s the body’s way of saying, “I am here, even if no one else notices.” You might feel this as a stubborn ache low in your back, a dull throb that wakes you at 3 a.m., the night heavy with the scent of cool rain pressing against your skin.

Above your shoulders, the trapezius muscle, broad and layered like a cape, becomes a battleground for misrepresentation. It tightens around the neck and upper back, holding the literal weight of carrying stories that aren’t yours or having your story twisted until it feels unrecognizable in your own mouth. This tension isn’t random. It’s a biological imprint of the body trying to protect you from the ongoing erasure and distortion of your experience. It’s the same muscle that tightens when you’re preparing to defend yourself, except here, it contracts in quiet desperation, a silent scream in muscle fibers.

The jaw, the masseter muscle, clenches as you rehearse the words you wish you had said—sharp, clear, undeniable. Instead, the tension sits like a stone lodged deep beneath your cheekbone, reminding you that your voice was sidelined, your fury dismissed as ugly or excessive. This is not pathology. This is biology. The body is a witness and a messenger, carrying the stories you could not tell aloud.

Teachers like Resmaa Menakem remind us that trauma and misrepresentation lodge in the body’s tissues, not just in the mind. The fascia, that dense connective web enveloping your muscles, remembers every time your truth was denied, every time your presence was minimized. It thickens, contracts, holds fast to the lies told about you, making it harder to move freely through the world. This embodied memory is why somatic practices matter—they help you negotiate the landscape of your lived experience, muscle by muscle, breath by breath, reclaiming the space your story deserves.

## The Media’s Distortion of Your Body

You know the feeling well: the tightening just beneath your collarbones, the subtle pinch in your trapezius and levator scapulae, like a slow squeeze that never quite releases. It’s late afternoon, and the pallid light slants through your window, falling on your skin like a cold judgment. You scroll, watch, listen. The media’s relentless churn of images and voices shapes a body that’s not yours—compressed, reformed, narrowed into ideals that serve someone else’s narrative. This is not just an assault on your mind; it’s a contraction in your fascia, a knotting in the masseter muscle that clenches your jaw without permission.

Resmaa Menakem teaches us that trauma is not only lodged in the mind but imprinted in the body’s tissues, passed down through generations, especially when racialized and gendered stress are involved. The media acts like a daily trauma trigger, a repetitive injury to your nervous system. When you see bodies like yours portrayed as angry, ugly, or out of control, the message seeps deep into your muscle membranes and connective tissue. Your psoas tightens, the primal muscle of fight and flight, preparing you to contract against a world that insists you are too much or not enough.

This isn’t pathology; it’s biology responding to a persistent threat. The body remembers what the jaw refuses to speak. Every time you absorb a stereotype—that your fury is “unfeminine” or “unseemly,” that your presence is a problem—you carry it in your body’s shape and tone. Your diaphragm may catch in shallow breaths, the very act of breathing made smaller, quieter, as if to disappear under the weight of those stories. The media’s distortion carves out space inside you where your anger should be free, instead turning it into a clenched fist beneath your ribs, a coil in your belly.

Your body is not a blank canvas for these narratives. It remembers who you are beneath the images—your ancestors’ resilience pulsing through your blood, the soft curves of your muscles ready to unfold. To resist the media’s grip, you have to start from the inside out, honoring the body’s wisdom and its urgent messages. This is where your healing begins: in the release of old tensions, the unwinding of contracted muscles, and the reclaiming of your full, fierce shape.

## The Biology of Being Unseen

You know that ache behind your sternum, that dull, persistent pressure that blooms when you scroll through the news or social media feeds where your truth is twisted or erased. This is not just a metaphorical bruising; it is your body registering the betrayal of being unseen, unheard, and gaslit by the very culture that should witness you. Gabor Maté, who tracks the deep currents of trauma and disconnection, reminds us that suppressed anger—especially the kind born from forced invisibility—does not dissipate into nothingness. Instead, it lodges in your tissues like a silent toxin.

When the media distorts your reality, replaying narratives that paint you as irrational, unstable, or worse, invisible, your nervous system reacts with a classic survival response. The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis floods your bloodstream with cortisol. Your diaphragm tightens; your intercostal muscles clamp down as your breath shortens without your permission. Your trapezius and levator scapulae muscles knot themselves into defensive armoring, bracing you for a threat that isn’t physical but no less real. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Maté teaches us that this chronic suppression—this forced silence—is a wound to your immune system. Your white blood cells, the vigilant sentinels that patrol your body, grow weary and confused. They cannot distinguish between real harm and the ongoing assault of psychological dissonance. The body’s resources get diverted from healing and regeneration to managing a state of constant alert. It’s as if your spleen and thymus, those quiet guardians of immunity, are whispering, “We’re tired of fighting shadows.”

Your anger, long deemed “ugly” or “inappropriate,” is in fact a vital signal. When you are pushed into a false narrative, when your experiences are denied by the media or society, your anger is the body’s desperate call to be recognized. This is the masseter muscle clenching at midnight as you lay awake, the subtle grinding in your jaw that remembers every slight, every erased word. It’s the psoas tightening in the early morning hours, a muscle that responds to the threat of invisibility by bracing you against the world.

To honor this anger is to honor your biology. To admit that the body knows when it’s being gaslit is the first step toward reclaiming your narrative—and your health. The story you were told to believe is a lie, but your body’s message is truth. It remembers. It feels. It waits for you to listen.

## Embodied Practice: Reclaiming Your Narrative

The body remembers every time your fury was dismissed, every moment you were told your anger was too much or too ugly. This isn’t just memory lodged in the mind; it’s inscribed in the fascia—the web of connective tissue that coats your muscles, bones, and organs like a second skin. That tightness behind your jaw, the hardened masseter, the clenched trapezius at the base of your neck—this is where your story waits, waiting for you to speak it aloud through your body, to reclaim it with flesh and breath.

Find a quiet corner where the light is soft—early morning is ideal, when the air still smells faintly of damp earth and promise. Sit on a firm but comfortable surface, your sit bones grounding you into the floor or chair. Let your knees fall naturally open or together, whatever feels less strained. Place your right hand gently over your heart, the left resting on your lower belly, just above the pelvis. This is no accident: your heart knows the truth of your rage, and your belly knows the depth of your survival.

Close your eyes and inhale through the nose, drawing breath deep into the diaphragm, expanding the belly under your left hand. Feel the subtle lift of the psoas inside, the gentle stretch of the anterior fascia. Exhale slowly through parted lips, releasing not just air but the dense, sticky tension gripping your chest and shoulders. With each exhale, imagine the fascia loosening, like old honey melting in warm water.

After three deep breaths, begin to hum softly on the outbreath, a low vibration that stirs the masseter and jawline. Let your jaw drop slightly, unclench the teeth, and feel the levator scapulae in your neck ease its grip. The hum is medicine here—its vibration travels through the bones and fascia, shaking loose the old stories of misrepresentation, the shame of being “too angry.”

Stay with this for five minutes. When you finish, gently place your hands together at heart center and bow your head, honoring the truth your body has just spoken. This practice is not about taming anger; it’s about listening to its language in your muscles and fascia, reclaiming the narrative that was never yours to lose. The body knows. The jaw remembers. You are not alone in this.

## Herbal Support for the Nervous System

You feel it in the nape of your neck, that relentless tension gripping the levator scapulae and trapezius like a vice, a physical echo of the endless scroll through incendiary headlines and performative outrage. The masseter clenches as your breath shallows, the diaphragm tightens, and your nervous system thrums with a static that doesn’t quiet when the screen dims. This is not weakness or failure — it’s the body’s unvarnished response to a relentless barrage of stimulation. To tend to this fraying, the herbal allies you choose must be precise, serious medicine for the central nervous system’s overworked fibers.

Wood Betony (Stachys officinalis) is one such ally, a plant revered by traditional European herbalists for its capacity to soothe the “nervous headaches” born from modern overwhelm. Its bitter, aromatic leaves infuse the blood with a subtle calm that sinks into the suboccipital muscles, releasing the stubborn knots where your head feels tethered to your shoulders. The herb’s action isn’t a sedative lull but a gentle rebalancing, reminding the nervous system that it can soften without losing alertness.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora), a North American staple, holds a different kind of quiet power. It works intimately on the vagus nerve, the body’s main parasympathetic highway, coaxing it back to a state of rest and digest. When your evening’s noise — the flashing notifications, the unrelenting news cycle — has you wired and unable to close your eyes, Skullcap’s bitter waters can untangle the diaphragm’s grip, smoothing breath and pulse alike. You might notice the subtle shift in your psoas, that deep core muscle that tenses with every fight-or-flight trigger. This is not a quick fix but a slow, whispered invitation to trust the body’s inherent resilience.

Milky Oats (Avena sativa), harvested at the perfect milky stage, offer a nourishing balm for the entire nervous system, especially the cerebral cortex that’s been overtaxed interpreting every outrage and alarm. Rich in calcium and magnesium, Milky Oats support nerve conduction and muscle relaxation, softening the masseter and releasing the jaw’s clenched memory of frustration. They are the herb of the tired, the overworked, the woman who was taught fury was ugly and so learned to swallow it until her body screamed.

Taken as tinctures or infused teas, these herbs are not magic but medicine — they work with your breath, your fascia, your very cells to rewrite the script of activation. For a trusted preparation, I often turn to [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20), a thoughtfully composed formula that blends these allies in exacting proportions, honoring the body’s wisdom and the complexity of your nervous system’s story. When you pour the dark amber tincture at dusk, feel the weight of your shoulders begin to soften, the tightness in your jaw relax in recognition: this is not pathology, this is biology, and it can be tended.

## FAQ: Navigating Rage at the News, Social Media, and Representation

You might notice that your rage flares most sharply when scrolling through the newsfeed late at night, the glow of the screen casting a bluish pallor on your skin, the masseter muscle clenched as headlines scream injustice. This is no accident. The body knows the weight of relentless exposure to trauma and outrage. Rage in response to the news is not a sign that you’re broken or overly sensitive; it’s a biological alarm, a call from your nervous system that this information is registering as threat. Your trapezius tightens, your diaphragm constricts, and your heart races because your body is trying to protect you from overwhelm. This is not pathology; this is biology.

Social media, with its endless scroll and curated outrage, can be a particularly insidious trigger. You find yourself caught in a loop of rage because the levator scapulae knots up, your breath shallows, and your nervous system hangs in a state of fight or flight long after you’ve put the phone down. You’re not just angry at the content; your body is flooded with stress hormones that make it hard to come back to rest. This is why grounding practices that focus on the body—feeling the soles of your feet on the earth, noticing the rise and fall of the diaphragm—are essential. Thich Nhat Hanh’s teachings on mindful breathing can be lifesaving here, as can the somatic awareness tools taught by Resmaa Menakem.

Representation—or the lack of it—unlocks a different kind of rage, often quieter but no less potent. When you see yourself erased or caricatured, the anger burrows deep into your psoas, that core muscle that holds your sense of safety and presence in the world. This rage is a message, a demand for visibility and respect. It’s important to honor it, not shove it down with a cup of chamomile or a quick scroll away. Karla McLaren’s work reminds us that anger is a messenger, not a monster to be tamed. You don’t have to carry this rage alone; it’s a clue to where healing must begin.

**Q:** Why do I feel so rageful when watching the news, even if I don’t know the people involved?

**A:** Your nervous system doesn’t distinguish well between direct and indirect threat. The images and stories activate your body’s alarm systems, tightening muscles like the trapezius and constricting your breathing. This is your body’s way of signaling danger, even if it’s mediated through a screen.

**Q:** How can I use social media without triggering constant rage?

**A:** It’s about setting boundaries that protect your nervous system. Notice where in your body the tension arises—maybe a clenched jaw or a knotted levator scapulae—and practice grounding. Take breaks, breathe deeply, and remember that your rage is a signal, not a failure.

**Q:** What does it mean when I feel rage about representation or erasure?

**A:** That rage is a deep, embodied message from your core muscles, especially the psoas. It expresses a need for acknowledgment and respect. Listening to that message is the first step to reclaiming your power and presence in the world.
